meta.label: Peruvian
meta.name: Peruvian
meta.subtitle: Bright acids, ají chiles, earthy potatoes, and coastal + Andean + Amazonian influences
meta.description: Peruvian cooking blends Indigenous Andean foundations (potatoes, corn, quinoa, ají chiles) with coastal seafood traditions and global influences (notably Spanish, African, Chinese-Cantonese “chifa,” and Japanese “nikkei”). SSA’s Peruvian profile emphasizes ají amarillo + ají panca, lime-forward marinades, punchy herb sauces, and starch-forward plates built around potatoes, rice, and corn.
heritageNotes: Common pantry anchors include ají amarillo and ají panca, Peruvian purple corn, and multiple potato varieties.
Flavor often balances acid (lime/vinegar), heat (ají), and savory depth (soy sauce in chifa, long-simmered broths, or roasted peppers).
Many dishes are built as: starch + protein + sauce/aji + crisp garnish.
meta.author: SSA Catalogs

source.attribution: General culinary knowledge synthesis (no single source).
source.author: SSA
source.notes: IDs reference SSA ingredient/method/equipment catalogs by convention.

overlapsWith.reason: Shared use of chiles, lime, and street-food patterns; distinct ají + potato emphasis in Peru.
Nikkei influence (seafood techniques, clean acid balance, soy/umami accents).
Chifa influence (soy sauce, stir-fries, fried rice, wok methods).

subprofiles.name: Coastal (Marino)
subprofiles.signatureNotes: Lime-forward seafood, ceviche, bright herb sauces, quick cooks.

subprofiles.name: Andean (Sierra)
subprofiles.signatureNotes: Potatoes, corn, quinoa; hearty stews and long braises; earthy pepper pastes.

subprofiles.name: Chifa
subprofiles.signatureNotes: Soy + garlic + ginger + scallion; wok cooking; fried rice/noodle patterns.

subprofiles.name: Nikkei
subprofiles.signatureNotes: Clean, precise acid balance; seafood; soy/citrus emulsions; fresh aromatics.

plannerDefaults.weeknightBias: Sauce + starch + protein is the default shortcut (aji sauce, rice/potatoes, quick-seared protein).

batchCookingBias.notes: Peruvian meals remix well: leftover rice becomes fried rice; sauces become sandwich spreads; braises become bowls.

signatureNotes: Ají chiles (especially ají amarillo and ají panca) build signature flavor more than generic “chili heat.”
Acid (lime/vinegar) is used boldly: marinades, ceviche, and bright finishing squeezes.
Savory depth can come from roasted pepper pastes, long braises, or chifa soy sauce accents.

tier0_minimum.description: Works for ‘Peru-inspired’ bowls and quick weeknight plates.

tier1_core.description: Core Peruvian flavor engines: ají amarillo/panca + key sauces.

tier2_traditional.description: Traditional/specialty staples: corn types, quinoa, key condiments.

tier3_specialty.description: Specialty items often harder to source; great for deeper authenticity.

notes: Keep acids bright: add lime/vinegar near the end unless curing seafood.
Ají pastes can scorch—cook gently in oil before adding liquids.
Chifa dishes benefit from very high heat and quick tosses; avoid overcrowding the pan.

dishArchetypes.label: Seafood + Acid (Ceviche Pattern)
dishArchetypes.description: Quick seafood prep driven by lime/acid, chili paste, onion, and crisp garnish; served with a starchy counterbalance.

dishArchetypes.label: Starch + Sauce + Protein
dishArchetypes.description: Potatoes or rice anchored with a creamy/spicy sauce and a protein topper (chicken, beef, eggs).
commonExamples: Papa a la huancaína (as a plate or side)

dishArchetypes.label: Chifa Stir-Fry + Rice
dishArchetypes.description: High-heat stir-fry with soy/garlic accents served with white rice; often includes onions, tomatoes, and fries (lomo saltado).

dishArchetypes.label: Stew + Starch
dishArchetypes.description: Comforting braises and stews served with rice/potatoes or corn/quinoa.

avoid_or_limit.label: Curry powder
avoid_or_limit.reason: Not typical to Peruvian flavor identity; can dominate and blur ají-based profiles.

proteinSubstitutions.notes: For ceviche-style dishes, use firm seafood that holds shape. Avoid very delicate fish unless handled gently.
Aji-style sauces and braises adapt well to turkey or pork.

sideSubstitutions.notes: Yuca works best when fried/boiled; sweet potato is classic with ceviche.
Quinoa is culturally aligned; cauliflower rice is a dietary adaptation.

thickenerSubstitutions.notes: Huancaína/aji sauces often use crackers or bread for body; gluten-free swaps work if blended thoroughly.

notes: If ají amarillo paste is unavailable, use a mild yellow chili paste or a blend of roasted yellow bell pepper + a small amount of hot chili, but keep color and fruitiness in mind.
Ají panca can be approximated with ancho paste + a touch of smoked paprika; avoid making it too smoky.

mealTemplates.label: Ají Bowl (Weeknight)

components.notes: Rice for a bowl; potatoes for a plated meal.

prepNotes: Batch the sauce on Sunday; use across bowls, sandwiches, and grilled proteins.

mealTemplates.label: Ceviche Plate (Fresh)
prepNotes: Keep seafood very cold; cut evenly; add lime right before serving for best texture.

safetyNotes.label: Ceviche Food Safety
notes: Use very fresh, properly handled seafood. Keep it refrigerated until the moment of prep.
Acid does not reliably ‘sterilize’ seafood—treat ceviche as raw seafood handling.
If serving to high-risk individuals, consider cooked seafood versions (poached shrimp) or fully cooked dishes.

safetyNotes.label: Ají Paste Handling
notes: Ají pastes can stain and irritate skin—use gloves if sensitive.
Toast/cook ají pastes gently in oil to prevent bitterness or scorching.

availabilityNotes.note: If ají amarillo is hard to source, use a mild yellow chili paste or roast yellow peppers and blend with a small amount of hot chili to keep color + fruitiness.
Prefer ancho paste for fruitiness; keep smokiness low unless the dish calls for it.

hallmarkProcesses.name: Ají Sauce Base (Blended)
hallmarkProcesses.notes: Cook ají paste briefly in oil, then blend with dairy/cheese/crackers or herbs; adjust acid at the end.

hallmarkProcesses.name: Leche de Tigre (Ceviche Base)
hallmarkProcesses.notes: Keep everything cold; add lime close to service for best texture.

signatureMethods: High-heat stir-fry with soy and aromatics (chifa).
Creamy ají sauces thickened with bread/crackers and cheese (huancaína/ají variants).
Acid cure for seafood with crisp onion and chile (ceviche/leche de tigre).

notes: A blender is the single most useful tool for Peruvian sauces (ají verde, huancaína, leche de tigre blends).
For chifa, a large skillet works if you don’t have a wok—just keep batches small for high heat.

core.name: Ceviche
core.notes: Fresh seafood, lime, onion, ají. Starch sides balance the acid.

core.name: Lomo Saltado
core.notes: High-heat beef stir-fry with onions/tomatoes, soy accents, served with fries and rice.

core.name: Ají de Gallina
core.notes: Creamy ají amarillo chicken sauce thickened with bread/crackers; served with rice and potatoes.

core.name: Papa a la Huancaína
core.notes: Boiled potatoes with huancaína sauce; classic starter/side.

core.name: Arroz Chaufa (Chifa Fried Rice)
core.notes: Use day-old rice; soy/garlic; eggs; scallions.

dessertsAndDrinks.name: Picarones
dessertsAndDrinks.notes: Fried squash/sweet potato rings with spiced syrup.

dessertsAndDrinks.name: Chicha Morada
dessertsAndDrinks.notes: Purple corn drink with fruit and spice notes.

dessertsAndDrinks.name: Pisco Sour
dessertsAndDrinks.notes: Classic cocktail (optional in SSA depending on household policies).

mealPatterns: Creamy ají sauce + rice/potatoes + shredded chicken.
Chifa stir-fry + rice (often with fries as a second starch).

typicalFlavorPairings.note: Ají amarillo’s fruitiness pairs well with creamy dairy and bright lime.
Chifa-style beef commonly uses soy with onion/tomato for savory-sweet balance.
Classic ceviche trio; keep onion crisp and lime fresh.

antiPairings.note: Over-smoky flavors can mask ají profiles; keep smoke subtle unless a specific dish calls for it.

ingredientRules.notes: Aim for yellow color + mild fruitiness. Add heat separately if needed.
Ancho approximates fruitiness; add a pinch of smoked paprika only if desired.
Blend thoroughly for a smooth sauce; adjust thickness with evaporated milk or stock.

methodRules.notes: Use a large skillet; cook in smaller batches to keep heat high.

rules.notes: Many dishes can be gluten-free by swapping soy sauce (use GF) and using GF crackers/bread in sauces.
Huancaína/ají creamy sauces are dairy-heavy; use cashew or oat cream substitutes but expect a different profile.

batchFriendly.name: Ají Verde Sauce
batchFriendly.notes: Make 3–5 days ahead; use on grilled chicken, bowls, sandwiches, roasted potatoes.

batchFriendly.name: Huancaína Sauce
batchFriendly.notes: Best within 3–4 days refrigerated; thin with evaporated milk as needed.

batchFriendly.name: White Rice
batchFriendly.notes: Cook extra; chill for arroz chaufa; freeze portions for quick bowls.

weeknightShortcuts: Use pre-cooked rice + seared protein + ají verde as a 15-minute bowl.
Roast potatoes while blending a sauce; add eggs or leftover chicken for a full meal.
For chifa, use day-old rice and high heat; keep sauce simple (soy + garlic + scallion).

notes: IDs in this profile assume SSA ingredient/method/equipment catalogs exist for common Peruvian staples and can be aliased if needed.
If your SSA ingredient catalog does not include ají/rocoto/huacatay IDs yet, add them or map aliases in the Peruvian ruleset for deterministic matching.
Peruvian plates often feel ‘right’ when a starch is present alongside sauce/acid; avoid planning too many ‘protein + veg only’ meals if aiming for authenticity.
Chifa patterns can share technique overlap with Chinese stir-fries but keep seasoning simpler and lime optional (not mandatory).
